2026-04-08T20:19:35.4934860Z [36;1m# Generate a secure random API key (360 bits of entropy, 40+ chars)[0m
2026-04-08T20:19:35.4935311Z [36;1m# Mask immediately to prevent timing vulnerabilities[0m
2026-04-08T20:19:35.4935688Z [36;1mAPI_KEY=$(openssl rand -base64 45 | tr -d '/+=')[0m

This appears to be a service-level geocoding accuracy concern rather than an SDK client defect.\n\n\u003cdetails\u003e\n\u003csummary\u003e📋 Issue Details\u003c/summary\u003e\n\n**Package:** `Azure.Maps.Search 2.0.0-beta.5`\n**Affected API:** Search/Geocoding API (address search / fuzzy search)\n**Scenarios:**\n- Querying an invalid or nonsensical address string results in a confident match (score 0.87) to a real street in Marseille, France\n- Expected behavior: low confidence score (\u003c 0.3) for a query that doesn't correspond to a real, unambiguous address\n\n**Root ask:** The author wants the geocoding confidence score to accurately reflect how poor a match is for clearly invalid or ambiguous input.\n\u003c/details\u003e\n\n\u003cdetails\u003e\n\u003csummary\u003e🔎 Debugging / Reproduction Notes\u003c/summary\u003e\n\nThe user provides a repro query: `\"  Cambodge Québec Cambodge  \"`. Azure Maps returns:\n- Score: `0.87` (high)\n- Address: Rue du Cambodge, Marseille, France\n\nThe author notes that Bing Maps correctly handles the same input with a much lower confidence.\n\n**Suggested investigation steps:**\n1. Reproduce by calling the Azure Maps Search API (fuzzy search / geocode endpoint) with query `\"Cambodge, Québec Cambodge\"` and observing the `score` field in the response.\n2. Determine whether this behavior is in the Maps Search SDK client (i.e., the SDK is misrepresenting or miscalculating the score) or is purely a service-side scoring decision returned from the Azure Maps REST API.\n3. If confirmed to be a service-side scoring issue, escalate to the Azure Maps service team; if an SDK interpretation issue exists, evaluate SDK handling of score/confidence values.\n4. Check if there is existing behavior documentation about how Maps search confidence scores are computed for partial or low-quality matches.\n\u003c/details\u003e\n\n\u003cdetails\u003e\n\u003csummary\u003e🏷️ Label Confidence\u003c/summary\u003e\n\n- **Category:** `Service` (excluded) — The SDK client appears to be functioning correctly: it sends the query and faithfully returns the service response. The issue is with the Azure Maps geocoding backend assigning an unexpectedly high confidence score to a nonsensical query. This makes \"Service\" the most accurate category, but it is excluded from automated triage.\n- **Service:** `Maps` — The issue explicitly names `Azure.Maps.Search` and describes Azure Maps geocoding behavior.\n- **Confidence:** Low (due to excluded label) — The service label is unambiguous (`Maps`), but since the most confident category prediction is `Service` (an excluded label requiring human judgment), the prediction cannot be applied automatically. Falling back to `needs-triage` only.\n\nOther categories considered:\n- `Client` — Rejected because the SDK itself is not misbehaving; it returns exactly what the service responds with. The issue is about the service's scoring accuracy, not a client library defect.\n\u003c/details\u003e\n\n\u003cdetails\u003e\n\u003csummary\u003e👥 Owner Routing\u003c/summary\u003e\n\n- **Matched CODEOWNERS entry:** N/A — Label prediction fell back to `needs-triage` due to excluded category label; owner routing (Step 5) was skipped.\n- **AzureSdkOwners:** none listed\n- **ServiceOwners:** For reference, `# ServiceLabel: %Maps` (line 339) lists `@dubiety` as ServiceOwner.
